comment.body: A squeeze can take time to develop. It doesn’t happen immediately. You typically need sustained upside momentum combined with rising volatility. A good historical reference is SPX in late August to early September 2020. That period illustrates how a slow build in positioning and volatility can eventually trigger a sharp squeeze.

comment.body: Because of the large amount of positive gamma, smart money understands that dealers are essentially drowning in it. Since volatility behaves reflexively, selling vol lowers realized vol, which encourages more vol selling. Any move away from this gamma heavy zone, whether up or down, is naturally suppressed.
comment.raw_body: Because of the large amount of positive gamma, smart money understands that dealers are essentially drowning in it. comment.body: If you want do understand this process, I would advice you to practice gamma scalping. Use a reliable cheap stock, like INTC. Buy a straddle and go do gamma scalping. You won't lose much money. You might even make some. But in the end, you understand the relationship between gamma, spot, theta, vega and IV.
comment.raw_body: If you want do understand this process, I would advice you to practice gamma scalping. Use a reliable cheap stock, like INTC. Buy a straddle and go do gamma scalping. You won't lose much money. You might even make some. But in the end, you understand the relationship between gamma, spot, theta, vega and IV.

comment.body: Btw...this was an inside day. Any vol you sold, would have been profitable. The daily straddle was price at $21 at BOD. If that straddle was sold and held until the close, it would be a full winner. There is more then long, or short. You can also be neutral.
